svg.title: 9 Vector Search Libraries Worth Knowing
svg.desc: A curated comparison of nine approximate-nearest-neighbor libraries, their strengths, best uses, and integrations, checked through July 10, 2026.
svg.text: ANN engines you embed, not databases you run — the components inside most vector databases.
Adoption and traction checked through July 10, 2026. Companion to “15 Vector Databases That Matter in Mid-2026”. Created by whitechno.github.io

g.text: The de facto standard library for dense-vector
2017). A composable toolbox of flat, IVF, PQ,
HNSW, Intel SVS / Vamana, and GPU indexes for
It serializes indexes and can memory-map IVF
data, but has no transactional database or
Best for: Large-scale offline / batch retrieval,
RAG backends, and embedding inside larger
became the default index in nearly every modern
without rebuilds. The library is tiny,
dependency-free, and near the top of
Best for: Fast in-memory ANN up to ~100M vectors
Young Rust vector index built on Google
distortion. Vectors are searchable the moment
they are ingested: no train step, no tuning, no
rebuilds, with SIMD kernels reported to beat
Very new: single maintainer, no named enterprise
Best for: Memory-constrained local / edge RAG,
search. A long-time top performer on
ann-benchmarks for CPU recall/QPS — the same
technology behind retrieval in Google Search and
YouTube; its AlloyDB index scales past 1B
vectors. Lives in the google-research monorepo;
Best for: High-QPS CPU serving of embeddings;
2019), now rewritten as DiskANN3 in Rust. Its
pluggable providers target memory, disk, Garnet,
and B-trees; Fresh / IP-DiskANN ideas support
pagination, and diversity-aware search. The old
C++ implementation is on cpp_main and no longer
actively maintained; the design lineage remains
Best for: Datasets far larger than RAM;
immutable index files that many processes share.
It powered Spotify's music recommendations for
years and was the gateway ANN library for a
generation of ML engineers. Static indexes; now
outpaced by HNSW-family methods and in
Spotify's modern successor to Annoy (2023) — an
HNSW library built on a hardened fork of hnswlib
and tuned for production ergonomics: thread-safe
first-class Python and Java bindings that read
the same index files. Spotify reports ~10x
speedups over Annoy at equivalent recall and
runs it across its ML systems. It saves and loads
indexes, but has no transactional serving layer.
Best for: Teams outgrowing Annoy; mixed JVM +
Python shops sharing one index format.
Single-header HNSW engine from Unum Cloud with
Java, Swift, Go, C#...). Differentiates on
int8 / binary quantization with zero heavy
easy to embed. It saves, loads, and memory-maps
Best for: Embedding vector search inside another
NVIDIA's CUDA vector search library (spun out of
RAPIDS RAFT), home of CAGRA — a graph index
designed for GPUs — plus IVF-Flat / IVF-PQ,
ScaNN, Vamana, and tiered-index building blocks.
NVIDIA reports up to ~12x faster FAISS index
builds than CPU HNSW at 95% recall. GPU-built
graphs convert to CPU-servable HNSW: build on
GPU, serve on CPU. Requires NVIDIA GPUs.
Best for: Massive index builds and GPU serving;

svg.text: Also notable: Intel SVS · KIOXIA AiSAQ · NGT (Yahoo Japan) · NMSLIB (hnswlib's ancestor) · Vamana / ParlayANN.

Logos: official marks of maintainer orgs (Meta, Google, Spotify — Annoy & Voyager, Microsoft, NVIDIA). hnswlib, Turbovec, and usearch publish no official logo — simplified recreations are shown for these three.
Adoption claims checked against engineering blogs, official docs & repositories through July 10, 2026.
